title: Drift decides a neutral gene
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: No selection, no mutation, no advantage — just chance. Start 200 identical populations at a 50/50 allele split and let each drift for 200 generations of random sampling. They fan out, and one by one an allele hits 100% (fixation) or 0% (loss); in a finite population, pure luck eventually wins. Smaller populations drift faster. This Wright–Fisher process is the null model of evolution — the baseline against which real selection has to prove itself.
